name: Cincinnati, Ohio
tagline: Ohio's third-largest city (~310K) running a council-manager government — its central IT, the Enterprise Technology Solutions (ETS) department, owns citywide infrastructure, applications, security, and the open-data platform across a $1.8B all-funds budget.
overview: Cincinnati is Ohio's third-largest city (roughly 310,000 residents) and the urban core of the Cincinnati metro. It operates under a council-manager form of government: a directly elected Mayor (Aftab Pureval) and a nine-member City Council set policy, while a professional City Manager (Sheryl Long) runs day-to-day operations and the ~6,000-employee workforce. Central technology is consolidated in the Enterprise Technology Solutions (ETS) department, which owns citywide IT strategy, infrastructure, enterprise applications, cybersecurity, emergency communications, and the regional law-enforcement data systems (RCIC/CLEAR). For a Dell SLG seller, Cincinnati is a mid-market municipal account where buying decisions concentrate in ETS and the City Manager's office, purchasing runs through the city's Office of Procurement, and — like most Ohio public entities — cooperative and state contract vehicles are common paths to award.

budget.intro: Cincinnati funds a sizable enterprise: the FY2026–2027 biennial budget runs more than $1.8B all funds, with the General Fund (the discretionary pool where most IT operating dollars sit) at roughly $593M. The city closed a ~$29.5M General Fund gap in the FY27 cycle largely through vacancy savings, which tends to tighten new discretionary spend.

stats.label: General Fund (FY27)
stats.note: Discretionary operating pool; ~1/3 of the budget

stats.label: FY27 GF gap closed
stats.note: Largely via vacancy savings — a headwind for new discretionary IT spend

notes: ETS is funded as a citywide service department; specific IT line items live in the operating budget rather than a single headline figure — confirm against the published budget documents.

Capital is buoyed by proceeds from the 2023 $1.6B Cincinnati Southern Railway sale (held in trust); ~$58M of railway revenue flows to FY27 capital, a potential source for one-time infrastructure refresh.

initiatives.name: Data-center zoning study (IDC Overlay #89)
initiatives.summary: In Jan 2026 the City Manager directed a zoning study on data-center impacts; Council established an Interim Development Control overlay, extended through end of 2026. Driven by AI/cloud demand — signals local sensitivity to power and land use that frames any on-prem/edge infrastructure conversation.

initiatives.name: Open data & analytics (CincyInsights / OPDA)
initiatives.summary: Cincinnati runs a mature open-data portal (Tyler Data & Insights) and an Office of Performance & Data Analytics in the City Manager's office. A data-modernization and analytics motion — storage, compute, and AI-readiness for civic data.

initiatives.name: ETS infrastructure & security modernization
initiatives.summary: ETS's Enterprise Infrastructure & Security division owns the city data center, fiber network, Microsoft 365, and cybersecurity controls. Core refresh territory for ISG (servers/storage), endpoint, and security infrastructure.

initiatives.name: Government restructuring (FY26)
initiatives.summary: City Manager Long announced a 2026 reorganization (dissolving DCED, creating an Office of Strategic Growth and a new resident-services department). Org change can reshape system needs and budget owners — track which departments consolidate.

procurement.intro: Cincinnati buys through its Office of Procurement (central purchasing for ~24 departments). Most public-sector IT buys in Ohio reach award via competitive solicitation or, more commonly for commodity technology, through cooperative and state contract vehicles — confirm the path before pursuing a deal.

vehicles.name: Cincinnati Office of Procurement
vehicles.detail: Central purchasing function led by an (Interim) Chief Procurement Officer; runs ITBs and RFPs and posts opportunities via the city portal / DemandStar. The front door for direct city awards.

vehicles.name: Ohio State Term Schedules / cooperative contracts
vehicles.detail: Ohio public entities routinely buy IT off state and cooperative vehicles to avoid running a full RFP. Confirm Dell coverage on the relevant Ohio cooperative/state contracts that Cincinnati can leverage.

vehicles.name: National cooperatives (e.g., Sourcewell, NASPO ValuePoint)
vehicles.detail: Common piggyback paths for municipal technology buys. Verify which co-ops the city's procurement code permits and that Dell/partner offerings are listed.

powerStructure.name: Aftab Pureval
powerStructure.title: Mayor
powerStructure.note: Directly elected; re-elected Nov 2025 by a wide margin. Sets policy direction and budget priorities with Council — the political sponsor for major initiatives.

powerStructure.name: Sheryl Long
powerStructure.title: City Manager
powerStructure.note: 18th City Manager; runs operations and ~6,000 employees, including ETS. The executive owner of citywide technology decisions and the FY26 reorg.

powerStructure.name: Sean Ware
powerStructure.title: IT Director, Enterprise Technology Solutions (ETS)
powerStructure.note: Leads ETS since Aug 2022 (30+ yrs IT); owns citywide IT strategy, infrastructure, applications, and IT governance. The primary technology relationship.

powerStructure.name: Enterprise Infrastructure & Security (ETS)
powerStructure.title: Network, data center & cybersecurity division
powerStructure.note: Owns data-center ops, fiber, M365, and security controls; no separately named CISO surfaced publicly — security sits within this division. Key buying center for ISG and security.

powerStructure.name: Office of Procurement
powerStructure.title: Central purchasing (Interim CPO: Laura Castillo)
powerStructure.note: Runs solicitations and contract awards for ~24 departments — the contracting path for any city deal.

news.title: Council adopts FY2027 budget update (June 18), ~$593M General Fund; ~$99.4M capital from railway proceeds; +$1.6M for police drone-first-responder expansion
news.impact: Vacancy savings and ~$20M in cuts closed the ~$29.5M gap, tightening net-new discretionary IT asks; capital is buoyed by railway proceeds (trust now ~$1.9B). The drone-first-responder line signals appetite for public-safety tech — a hook for edge compute, storage, and data-management conversations. Lead with cooperative-vehicle and refresh plays.

news.title: Government reorganization goes operational — Office of Strategic Growth and Department of Opportunity & Resident Services (DOORS) now stood up
news.impact: The DCED dissolution announced in March is now live; new departments mean new system owners and budget lines — re-map stakeholders before forecasting multi-department deals.

news.title: City Council extends data-center zoning restrictions (IDC Overlay #89) through end of 2026
news.impact: Local sensitivity to data-center power/land use; frame infrastructure conversations around efficiency and existing-footprint modernization. Zoning study will produce a data-center definition and possible permanent code amendments — watch for the outcome.

news.title: City Manager directs data-center impact zoning study
news.impact: AI/cloud-driven demand prompts policy review — signals where the city's attention sits on compute and power infrastructure.

news.title: Mayor Aftab Pureval re-elected
news.impact: Continuity at the top of city government — stable policy and budget direction for multi-year engagements.

dellActions: Build the ETS relationship first — IT Director Sean Ware and the Enterprise Infrastructure & Security division are the buying center for ISG (servers/storage), endpoint, and security infrastructure refresh.
Confirm Dell coverage on Ohio state/cooperative contracts (and national co-ops the city permits) so there's a low-friction award path that avoids a full RFP.

Lead with efficiency and existing-footprint modernization, not net-new data-center expansion — the active data-center zoning restrictions and a tight FY27 General Fund both favor consolidation/refresh framing.
Tie a data and AI-readiness story to the open-data/analytics motion (CincyInsights, OPDA) — storage and compute that modernize civic data without triggering the data-center policy debate.
Track the FY26 government reorg to confirm which departments and budget owners survive consolidation before forecasting multi-department deals.
